nativeRange.habitat: Found in tropical reef environments across the Indo-Pacific and Red Sea, from shallow sunlit reef flats down to around 40 metres. Smaller and younger specimens tend to form clusters in well-lit, shallow zones, while large solitary adults are more often encountered in deeper, dimly lit areas where their tentacles remain elongated rather than bubble-tipped.

decorPreferences: stable rockwork base for foot attachment
open sandy area adjacent to rockwork
powerhead covers or guards (critical safety — anemone will roam)

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae symbiosis; supplemental target feeding once or twice weekly is generally recommended. Accepts chopped silversides, krill, mysis shrimp, marine shrimp, mussel, and squid. Food should be placed directly on the tentacles using tongs or a feeding stick. Feeding frequency can be reduced or eliminated when a clownfish host actively feeds the anemone waste.

goodWith: Premnas biaculeatus (Maroon / Gold Stripe Maroon Clownfish)
reef fish (non-aggressive species that avoid the anemone)
avoidWith: other anemone species (contact causes stinging and chemical warfare)
aggressive fish that may harass or damage anemone tentacles
unguarded powerheads or intakes (anemone wandering is a major tank hazard)

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso; 5792x5792. In-situ green colour morph clump with clearly bubble-tipped, white-speckled tentacles — best match for the green body + bubble-tip diagnostic features; sharp, whole animal in frame.
Diego Delso; 5095x5095. Close detail of translucent green bubble-tipped tentacles showing the swollen bulb morphology; slightly darker/softer than the recommended pick.
Nhobgood; 2048x1536. In-situ anemone with bubble tips hosting several tomato clownfish — illustrates the species' signature clownfish-hosting role; anemone colour is a paler grey-green here.

summary: The Green Bubble Tip Anemone (Entacmaea quadricolor) is widely considered the most popular reef-aquarium anemone, prized for hosting clownfish and occurring in vivid color morphs. An intermediate-level species, it requires a mature, stable tank and guarded powerheads throughout the aquarium.
careNotes:
Entacmaea quadricolor is generally considered the most reef-aquarium-friendly anemone and is the host of choice for 14 anemonefish species. It appears in multiple color variants — green (most common from Fiji/Tonga stock), rose/pink, red/orange, and brown — and in the trade these are often sold under separate names (Rose BTA, Watermelon BTA, etc.). All are the same species and share identical husbandry requirements.

Bubble-tip morphology: Healthy specimens held under adequate lighting tend to display the characteristic bulbous, swollen tentacle tips that give the species its common name. Under low light or when hungry, tentacles elongate and appear stringy rather than bubble-tipped. Tentacles appearing stringy is an early sign that lighting or feeding needs adjustment.

Shallow vs. deep form: In the wild, smaller specimens form shallow-water colonies in bright sunlight while large solitary adults inhabit deeper, dimly lit zones with stringy tentacles. Aquarium specimens typically behave as the colonial shallow form.

Lighting: Generally described as requiring moderate to high intensity to maintain zooxanthellae health. High PAR (roughly 150–350+ µmol/m²/s) is broadly advised by advanced hobbyists, with acclimation recommended when placing under intense LEDs or metal halide fixtures. Insufficient lighting will cause bleaching over time.

Water flow: Moderate, indirect flow is generally recommended. Avoid directing powerhead jets directly at the anemone. The anemone should gently sway but not be battered.

Wandering — the most critical tank hazard: BTAs will relocate frequently, especially when unhappy with light or flow conditions. An anemone that contacts an unguarded powerhead impeller will be shredded, potentially fouling the tank and harming other inhabitants. All powerheads and intake lines must be covered with foam guards or screens before introducing a BTA. This is the single most common cause of catastrophic BTA-related tank loss.

Allelopathy and aggression: BTAs will sting and kill corals or other anemones they contact during movement. They do not actively hunt tankmates but their nematocysts are potent enough to injure fish that blunder into their tentacles. Allow several centimeters of clearance from all neighboring corals.

Tank maturity: A well-established tank (commonly advised minimum 6 months of cycling) is generally recommended before adding a BTA, as they are sensitive to parameter swings that a new tank is more prone to.

Feeding: Target feeding once or twice weekly with small pieces of meaty marine foods (silversides, krill, mysis, shrimp, clam) placed directly on tentacles. A hosting clownfish reduces but does not eliminate the supplemental feeding need. Do not overfeed — bloated or persistently closed specimens may be suffering from water quality issues rather than hunger.

Asexual reproduction (splitting): A healthy, well-fed BTA in good conditions may periodically split longitudinally into two clones. This is normal and can be a sign of good health, though it does occasionally indicate stress. Both daughter anemones will re-establish after splitting.

Color morphs: 'Rose BTA' and 'Watermelon BTA' are trade names for color variants (pink/rose and green-pink respectively) of the same species. 'Green BTA' describes specimens with a green body. Husbandry is identical across all color forms.

Wild-type BTAs are believed to be very long-lived; aquarium specimens kept in stable conditions are routinely reported to survive for decades.
breedingNotes: The Green Bubble Tip Anemone reproduces asexually in aquaria through a process called longitudinal fission, in which a healthy, well-fed specimen splits lengthwise into two genetically identical individuals. Both daughter anemones typically remain contracted for several days before reopening and re-establishing. In the wild, sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning — the release of eggs and sperm into the water column — with spawning observed between January and April in Australian populations. The resulting free-swimming larvae (planulae) settle onto reef substrate to form new individuals. Deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Green Bubble Tip Anemone
primary.notes: Primary source. Extracted water parameters (temperature 72–78°F, salinity SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12), adult size 12 in, minimum tank 30 gal, care level Moderate, temperament Semi-Aggressive, lighting Moderate to High, flow Medium. Second product page (Watermelon BTA) confirmed identical parameters.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Entacmaea quadricolor
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family: Actiniidae, Order: Actiniaria), native range (tropical Indo-Pacific including Red Sea), size confirmation (up to 30 cm), and complete list of 14 symbiotic clownfish host species by scientific name. Also provided depth-form distinction (shallow clustered vs. deep solitary) and reproduction details (broadcast spawning Jan–Apr, longitudinal fission).

additional.name: Aquarium Source – Bubble Tip Anemone
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (72–82°F), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity SG 1.023–1.025, kH 8–12, size ~12 in. Confirmed compatible clownfish list (Percula, Gold Maroon, Australian, Allard's, Barber's, Maroon, Tomato, Clark's, Skunk, Red Saddleback). Feeding: 3× weekly maximum. Minor disagreement on temperature upper bound (82°F vs LiveAquaria's 78°F).

additional.name: Saltwater Fish Keeping – Bubble Tip Anemone
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (25–28°C), pH (8.2–8.4), salinity SG 1.023–1.025, kH 8–11, adult size 30 cm, minimum tank 30 gal / 114 L. Confirmed carnivore diet (finely chopped seafood 1–2× weekly). Lifespan cited as approximately 80 years in captivity. Confirmed aggressive sting and wandering hazard.
